title: Dog Foxtail Grass in Ear: Urgency Guide and Triage Steps
meta_desc: Foxtails in a dog's ear are high-urgency emergencies. Learn why they move deeper, the risks involved, and how triage photos assist veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: A foxtail in a dog’s ear is a high-priority emergency that requires professional removal to prevent eardrum perforation and deep infection. Do not attempt to remove the seed yourself, as the barbed structure causes it to move deeper into the canal when touched.

What is a foxtail and why is it dangerous if it gets in my dog's ear?

A foxtail is a barbed seed head from certain types of grasses, such as wild barley. These seeds are uniquely shaped like arrows, designed to move in only one direction: forward. When a foxtail enters a dog's ear canal, the microscopic barbs prevent it from falling out. Instead, every shake of the dog's head or touch to the ear pushes the seed deeper into the sensitive ear canal.

How urgent is a foxtail in my dog's ear?

  • A foxtail stuck deep in the ear is a high-priority veterinary emergency because the seed is designed to migrate forward.
  • The seed can quickly reach the eardrum, causing painful perforation, or travel into the middle ear and cause severe infections.
  • Professional removal is required and often involves sedation and specialized tools, such as an otoscope and alligator forceps, to ensure no barbs are left behind.

How does taking a photo help the veterinary triage process?

  • Taking a clear photo of the ear or any visible discharge allows a veterinary professional to assess the severity of inflammation and the presence of external barbs.
  • A photo helps the clinic prioritize your arrival and prepare the necessary equipment to provide immediate relief without you needing to probe the sensitive ear.
  • Visual information ensures the team is ready for professional extraction, which is vital since you should never attempt to pull a deep seed yourself.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Foreign bodies, such as foxtails, should be suspected as a cause of otitis externa. Key to treatment is identifying and addressing all primary and secondary causes and predisposing factors. Management of pain or pruritus must be included in the initial treatment protocol. Before flushing the ear canal, samples for cytologic evaluation and culture should be obtained. In chronic cases where the canal is too stenotic to examine, systemic glucocorticoids may reduce swelling enough to allow examination. If sedation is not needed, samples for ear diagnostic tests should be collected.

Chapter: Dermatology, Neurology, Diagnostic Imaging

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 529)

faqs.question: Can I use tweezers to pull it out at home?
faqs.answer: No. Attempting to remove a foxtail yourself often results in pushing it deeper or causing the seed to break, leaving dangerous fragments inside the ear canal.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of a foxtail in the ear?
faqs.answer: Signs include sudden, frantic head shaking, tilting the head to one side, scratching at the ear, whining, or localized swelling and discharge.

faqs.question: Can a foxtail in the ear go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No, foxtails do not dissolve and cannot be worked out by the dog. They will only continue to migrate deeper until they are surgically or professionally removed.
